content.problemStatement:
The growth of Computer Science has forced the scientific community to award Nobel Prize in CS starting from this year.

Chef knows that the Nobel community is going to award the prize to that person whose research is different from others (ie. no other researcher should work on the same topic). If there are multiple such people, who work on unique topics, then they will all share the prize. It might also happen that no one wins this time.

Chef also knows the NNN researchers which the community who will be considered for the prize, and the topics in which each of them work.

In total the CS field can be divided into MMM broad topics. Given the topics in which each of the NNN researchers are working on, in the form of an array AAA, and given that Chef can master any topic instantly, find whether he can win the prize. That is, can the Chef choose to work on some topic which will guarantee that he will win the prize? Chef doesn't mind sharing the prize with others.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
Each testcase contains of two lines of input.
First line contains two space separated integers NNN, MMM, number of researchers excluding Chef and total number of fields respectively.
Second line contains NNN space separated integers A1,A2,…ANA_1, A_2, \ldots A_NA1​,A2​,…AN​, research topic of the researchers.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line answer, "Yes" if Chef can win the prize and "No" if not.


You may print each character of each string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes" and "YES" will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
Sum of M over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and M — the number of researchers (excluding Chef) and the total number of possible topics. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN, where Ai is the topic of the i‑th researcher (1 ≤ Ai ≤ M).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if Chef can choose a topic that guarantees he will be a unique‑topic researcher (and thus win the prize), otherwise output "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: All 4 topics are already taken, so Chef cannot pick a unique one → No.\nTest case 2: Only topics 1,2,4 are taken, topic 3 is free → Chef can pick 3 → Yes.\nTest case 3: Only topic 4 is taken, many topics are free → Yes.
Distinct topics are {1,2}. Since M=5, there are free topics (e.g., 3) → Chef can win.
All three topics 1,2,3 are already present, so no free topic exists.

solution.approach: For each test case we only need to know how many different topics are already taken by the N researchers. If the number of distinct topics is strictly less than M, there exists at least one unused topic; Chef can pick it and his topic will appear exactly once, so he wins. Otherwise every topic from 1 to M is already taken and Chef cannot be unique. We can compute the number of distinct topics using a hash set (or a boolean array) while reading the Ai values.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of researchers in that test case. Over all test cases the total time is O(ΣN) ≤ 10^6.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(min(N, M)) for the set/boolean array storing seen topics.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); M = int(next(it))
        seen = set()
        for _ in range(N):
            seen.add(int(next(it)))
        out_lines.append('Yes' if len(seen) < M else 'No')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
